coredata.dc:title: A turn-on/off fluorescent sensor based on nano-structured Mg-Al layered double hydroxide intercalated with salicylic acid for monitoring of ferric ion in human serum samples
coredata.dc:creator: Abdolmohammad-Zadeh, Hossein
Zamani-Kalajahi, Monireh
coredata.dc:description: The present work aimed to provide a novel chemosensor for ferric ion detection in human serum samples using nano-structured Mg-Al layered double hydroxide intercalated with salicylic acid (Mg-Al LDH–SA) as a sensitive fluorescence probe. The Mg-Al LDH–SA nanomaterial was synthesized and characterized via Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction analysis, field emission scanning electron microscopy, dynamic light scattering and energy dispersive spectroscopy. The synthesized nanomaterial was applied to determine ferric ions by measuring the fluorescence intensity of Mg-Al LDH–SA sol solution at λem = 404 nm with excitation at λex = 287 nm. In the presence of ferric ions, the fluorescence intensity decreases owing to the formation of a stable complex between ferric ions and salicylic acid. Several important parameters affecting the analytical signal were studied and optimized. Under optimum conditions, the calibration graph was linear in the concentration range of 0.07–100 μmol L−1 with a corresponding detection limit of 26 nmol L−1. The intra- and inter-day relative standard deviations (n = 6 and ferric ion concentration; 50 μmol L−1) were 3.4% and 4.5%, respectively. The method was successfully employed to assess ferric ion in several human serum samples with relative recovery values between 98.7 and 104.6% for the spiked samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ferric ion
Mg-Al layered double hydroxide nano-particles
Salicylic acid
Turn-on/off fluorescent chemical sensor
Serum samples

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Mg-Al LDH intercalated with salicylic acid (SA)
Measurement of the fluorescence quantum yield
Mechanism of the Fe3+ ion detection
Method validation and analysis of real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of (a) Mg-Al-Cl LDH (Mg2+: Al3+ ratio of 4: 1) and (b) Mg-Al LDH–SA.
FT-IR spectra of (a) Mg-Al-Cl LDH, (b) pure SA and (c) Mg-Al LDH–SA.
FE-SEM images of Mg-Al LDH–SA nanomaterial at 75000 × m (up) and 150000 × m (bottom) magnifications.
(a) Particle size distribution of Mg-Al LDH–SA obtained from DLS analysis and, (b) Zeta potential diagram of Mg-Al LDH–SA nanomaterial.

Effect of (a) pH, and (b) initial SA concentration on the fluorescence intensity difference of Mg-Al LDH–SA in the absence and presence of 50 μmol L−1 Fe3+ ions at λem = 404 nm with excitation at λex = 287 nm.

The fluorescence emission spectra of (a) Mg-Al-Cl LDH; (b) SA solution (the same concentration as used for the Mg-Al LDH–SA synthesis conditions); (c) Mg-Al LDH–SA in the presence of Fe3+ ions, and (d) Mg-Al LDH–SA without adding any Fe3+ ion. Other conditions: Fe3+ ion concentration; 70 μmol L−1, and λex = 287 nm.

(a) The fluorescence emission spectra of Mg-Al LDH–SA nanomaterial in the presence of Fe3+ ions at a concentration range of 0–500 μmol L−1 with excitation at λex = 287 nm, and (b) Stern–Volmer plots for the quenching of Mg-Al LDH–SA fluorescence by Fe3+ ions in the concentration range of 0.07–100 μmol L−1 at 298 K (up), and 313 K (bottom). Other conditions: λem = 404 nm, and λex = 287 nm.

(I) The fluorescence emission spectra of the deproteinized human serum sample before (a) and after adding 5, 10, and 15 μmol L−1 standard solution of Fe3+, respectively (b–d), (II) calibration graph of the standard addition method, and (III) Stern–Volmer plot for the quenching of Mg-Al LDH–SA fluorescence by Fe3+ ions in the human serum medium. Other conditions: λem = 404 nm, and λex = 287 nm.
EDS analysis results (atomic percent (%)) of Mg-Al-Cl LDH and Mg-Al-Cl LDH–SA nanomaterials.
Tolerance limits of interfering ions in the determination of 50 μmol L−1 Fe3+ ions.

row.entry: Na+, K+, Ag+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Hg2+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of analytical characteristics of the presented method with some reported optical chemosensors for the determination of Fe3+ ion.

row.entry: N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots
B, N, S co-doped carbon dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of Fe3+ ion in human serum samples and validation results of the analyte by atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) method compared with the presented method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Mean of three experiments ± standard deviation.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Obtained from the Iranian Blood Transfusion Research Center, Tabriz, Iran.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Obtained from Danesh Pathobiology Lab., Tabriz, Iran.

head.ce:title: A turn-on/off fluorescent sensor based on nano-structured Mg-Al layered double hydroxide intercalated with salicylic acid for monitoring of ferric ion in human serum samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical Spectroscopy Research Lab., Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University, 35 Km Tabriz-Marageh Road, P.O. Box 53714-161, Tabriz 5375171379, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present work aimed to provide a novel chemosensor for ferric ion detection in human serum samples using nano-structured Mg-Al layered double hydroxide intercalated with salicylic acid (Mg-Al LDH–SA) as a sensitive fluorescence probe. The Mg-Al LDH–SA nanomaterial was synthesized and characterized via Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction analysis, field emission scanning electron microscopy, dynamic light scattering and energy dispersive spectroscopy. The synthesized nanomaterial was applied to determine ferric ions by measuring the fluorescence intensity of Mg-Al LDH–SA sol solution at λem = 404 nm with excitation at λex = 287 nm. In the presence of ferric ions, the fluorescence intensity decreases owing to the formation of a stable complex between ferric ions and salicylic acid. Several important parameters affecting the analytical signal were studied and optimized. Under optimum conditions, the calibration graph was linear in the concentration range of 0.07–100 μmol L−1 with a corresponding detection limit of 26 nmol L−1. The intra- and inter-day relative standard deviations (n = 6 and ferric ion concentration; 50 μmol L−1) were 3.4% and 4.5%, respectively. The method was successfully employed to assess ferric ion in several human serum samples with relative recovery values between 98.7 and 104.6% for the spiked samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Ferric (Fe3+) ion is one of the metal ions that plays important role in biological systems so that without it practically all life would cease to exist. It is an essential metal ion in many enzymatic electron transfers, oxygen transportation, oxygen metabolism, oxidation reactions and hemoglobin synthesis. Normal human serum iron level has been reported in the range of 14–32 μmol L−1 [1]. Iron deficiency or anemia is a condition resulting from too little iron in the body [2]. Iron deficiency and excess in the human body can induce a variety of diseases [3]. Therefore, it is of great importance to develop simple and sensitive analytical methods for the detection of Fe3+ ion in clinical, environmental and industrial areas. Different analytical techniques have been employed for the determination of Fe3+ ion either in biological or in water samples [4–9]. However, most of the used methods suffer from several limitations, which mainly limit their practical applications, such as high-cost, less sensitive and low anti-interference ability.

Fluorescence spectrometry is widely used in the quantitative analysis due to its high selectivity, sensitivity, and its relatively low cost. Development of fluorescence sensing materials for detecting of Fe3+ ion can offer a unique combination of key merits including simple and rapid operation and high sensitivity. Advances in nanoscience and nanotechnology have led to the development of new nanomaterial assays for Fe3+ ions detection. So far, N-doped graphene quantum dots [10], fluorescent gold nanoclusters [11,12], L-cysteine capped Fe3O4@ZnO core-shell nanoparticles [13], carbon dots [14–17], silver nanoclusters [18] and nanoporous silica [19] materials have been used as a sensing probe for Fe3+ ions in different matrices.

Layered double hydroxides (LDHs) have received significant attention in recent years owing to their flexible structures like anion exchange capacity, excellent biocompatibility, pH-dependent solubility, and high chemical stability that make them promising materials for a variety of practical applications [20]. LDHs have been widely used in different areas including drug delivery, polymer science, catalysis, electrochemistry, photochemistry and separation technology [21–24]. LDHs are a significant group of host–guest nanostructured materials containing of positively charged metal hydroxide sheets with interlayer galleries [25]. The interlayer galleries of LDHs contain both interlayer anions and water molecules and there is a complex network of hydrogen bonds between layer hydroxyl groups, anions and water molecules. LDHs have comparatively feeble interlayer bonding, and anions could be intercalated between their layers to compensate their structural positive charge and possess exchange capabilities that can readily exchange with organic and inorganic anions [26]. Hence, in recent years, the use of LDHs as host materials has become of increasing interest because of creating inorganic-organic host-guest hybrid nanostructures for fabrication of various optical sensors [27–30]. LDH matrix provides a special structure for intercalated fluorophores such as immobilization of fluorophore in a stable environment improving fluorescence efficiency and optical stability of the fluorophore [29,30]. However, although a colorimetric dispersive solid-phase extraction method has been recently reported for the determination of Fe2+ ion using dissolvable bathocuproine disulfonic acid intercalated LDH nanosheet [31], to the best of our knowledge, the use of LDHs for spectrofluorometric determination of Fe3+ ions has not been reported yet. This motivates us to follow the incorporation of fluorescent molecule as the guest into the LDH host matrix to fabricate a novel chemosensor for Fe3+ ion detection.

Salicylic acid (SA), 2-hydroxybenzoic acid, is an organic acid that used in therapeutic treatment of rheumatism. SA has been previously intercalated into hydrotalcite-like compounds for control release formulation [32]. Also, it has been used as an emission reagent for spectrofluorometric determination of Fe3+ ions in aqueous solutions [9]. However, the low sensitivity and narrow linear range have limited its application in complex biological samples. Consequently, in this study, a highly fluorescent nanomaterial based on nano-structured Mg-Al LDH intercalated with SA was introduced as a sensitive chemosensor for the monitoring of Fe3+ ion in biological samples. The detection mechanism is based on the formation of a stable complex between Fe3+ ions and SA intercalated into the LDH, leading to a decrease in fluorescence intensity of Mg-Al LDH–SA nanomaterial owing to reduction of fluorescent SA molecules in the Mg-Al LDH–SA structure. Several important factors affecting the analytical signal were studied and optimized. The method was effectively used to determine Fe3+ ion in numerous human serum samples.

All chemicals used were of high analytical purity grade. LDH precursors i.e., magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2.6H2O) and aluminum chloride hexahydrate (AlCl3.6H2O), sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), salicylic acid (SA), ethanol, iron (III) nitrate hexahydrate (Fe(NO3)3.6H2O), trichloroacetic acid (TCA), tyrosine and nitric acid (HNO3) and other salts used for the interference study were all obtained from Merck manufacture (Darmstadt, Germany). The solutions were prepared with deionized water purchased from Ghazi Pharmaceutical Company (Tabriz, Iran). A 0.1 mol L−1 stock solution of Fe3+ ion and other cations used for the interference study were prepared by dissolving the appropriate amount of their nitrate salts in deionized water. A 1000 mg L−1 stock solution of SA was prepared by dissolving of 0.100 g SA powder in 2 mL of ethanol and diluting to 100 mL with deionized water. To calculate the quantum yields, a 1000 mg L−1 stock solution of tyrosine was prepared by dissolving of 0.100 g tyrosine powder in 100 mL of 0.05 mol L−1 NaOH solution. The stock solutions were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C and protected from light. The working solutions were prepared daily by appropriate dilution of stock solutions with deionized water. All the plastic and glassware employed for the analysis were soaked in 20% nitric acid and cleaned prior to use.

Fluorescence intensity measurements were performed using a FP–6200 spectrofluorometer (JASCO Corporation, Tokyo, Japan). A flame atomic absorption spectrometer (model SpectrAA 220 Varian, Australia) was used for the determination of iron via a standard method. To identify the crystal structure of the prepared nanomaterials, XRD measurements were carried out on a Bruker D8 Advance (model AXS, Germany) X-ray powder diffractometer between 6 and 75° generated at 40 kV and 35 mA. Also, FT-IR spectra were recorded on a Vector 22 (Bruker, Germany) Fourier transform infrared spectrometer in the wavenumber range between 4000 and 400 cm−1. The size and morphology of Mg-Al LDH–SA were characterized by a field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) model MIRA3 TESCAN-XMU (Brno, Czech Republic). The elemental composition of Mg-Al LDH and Mg-Al LDH–SA nanomaterials were studied using energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) attached to the FE-SEM. Zeta potentials determinations and dynamic light scattering (DLS) analyses were performed with a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS analyzer (model ZEN3600, UK). UV–Vis spectra were recorded on a T80 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (PG Instrument Ltd, UK). The pH values were measured with a Metrohm pH-meter (model 827, Switzerland). An electronic analytical balance model ABT 100-5 M (Kern Co., Germany) was used for weighting the solid materials.

Mg-Al-Cl LDH was synthesized by a co-precipitation method at pH 10 [33,34]. The preparation was carried out under a N2 atmosphere to exclude the aqueous CO2. Two solutions were prepared; solution A containing 200 mL of 0.8 mol L−1 Mg2+ and 0.2 mol L−1 Al3+ (from their chloride salts) and solution B containing 200 mL of 1 mol L−1 NaOH and 1 mol L−1 NaCl. The prepared solutions were simultaneously added into 100 mL of 80 °C of deionized water under vigorous stirring for two hours. The pH of the resulted slurry solution was adjusted at 10 by adding 2 mol L−1 NaOH solution. The slurry was then stirred at 70 °C for 24 h under a N2 atmosphere. The resultant was filtered and washed exhaustively with deionized water until the washings were neutral. Finally, the precipitate was dried at 70 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Mg-Al LDH intercalated with salicylic acid (SA)
ce:section.ce:para: Positively charged LDHs can absorb anionic species from aqueous systems by ion-exchange, surface adsorption, and reconstruction. Mg-Al LDH–SA nanomaterial samples were prepared by the method described elsewhere [32] with some modification and using the following procedure: 0.5 g of the synthesized Mg-Al-Cl LDH was added into 100 mL of 100 mg L−1 SA aqueous solution under a N2 atmosphere and vital magnetic stirring. The reaction mixture was allowed to proceed at room temperature for 24 h. The precipitate was collected by filtration, washed with deionized water and dried in an oven at 65 °C for 24 h.

Human serum samples were attained from the Iranian Blood Transfusion Research Center (Tabriz, Iran) and seven volunteers who were informed about the objectives of the project and signed an approval form permitted by the ethical committee of Tabriz University of Medical Sciences in Danesh Pathobiology Lab. (Tabriz, Iran). All serum samples were individually transferred into small tubes and frozen at −4 °C until analysis time. On the day of analysis, frozen serum samples were defrosted at room temperature and vortexed to ensure homogeneity. The procedure for removing of serum proteins was carried out according to the literature [35]. Briefly, an aliquot of 300 μL serum sample was transferred into a 15 mL cylindrical centrifuge tube and 375 μL of deionized water and 225 μL of 20% (w/v) TCA were added and mixed by vortexing. Afterward, it was covered with an aluminum cap and heated for 15 min in a water bath maintained at 90–95 °C. After cooling, it was then centrifuged (still covered with the aluminum cap) at 4000 rpm for 15 min. Then, the aluminum cap was removed, and clear supernatant (300 μL) was transferred into a small glass vial for use in the next step.

An aliquot of 300 μL of clear supernatant was transferred into a glass vial. Afterward, 1 mL of 0.1 mol L−1 NaOH solution was added into the solution and diluted with deionized water up to 3 mL while the pH was adjusted to around 7. Then, 2.0 mg of Mg-Al LDH–SA nanomaterial was added into the solution, and the mixture was sonicated for 5 min at room temperature before the spectral measurements. The fluorescence intensity of the samples was determined at λem = 404 nm with excitation at 287 nm wavelength. The both of excitation and emission slit widths were set to 5 nm, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded with a scan speed of 250 nm min−1. All fluorescence measurements were performed at 25 ± 0.2 °C controlling with a Peltier thermostat cell holder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of the fluorescence quantum yield
ce:section.ce:para:
Determination of the fluorescence quantum yields was achieved using L-tyrosine (ΦR = 0.14) as a standard at an excitation wavelength of 275 nm. For calculation of the quantum yields, aqueous solutions of tyrosine and SA were prepared and LDH–SA was dispersed in deionized water. The concentration of all the samples were adjusted with their absorbance less than 0.1 at 287 nm and their fluorescence spectra were recorded under the same excitation wavelength at 287 nm. The fluorescence quantum yields were calculated according to the following equation:
(1) ΦR/Φs = (IRAS ŋ2 R)/(ISARŋ2 S)
where “R” and “S” refer to reference (tyrosine) and samples (SA and LDH–SA), respectively. Φ is the fluorescence quantum yield, I is the fluorescence emission intensity, A is the UV–Vis absorbance and ŋ is the refractive index of the solution (pure solvents were assumed).

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis is a valuable tool for monitoring of intercalation process, because the size of the intercalated ion is one of the factors influencing the interlayer spacing of the hydrotalcite compounds [32]. Thus, the intercalation of SA into the layers of host LDH can be confirmed by increasing of the interlayer spacing of resulted Mg-Al LDH–SA. So, identification of the crystalline phases of LDH–SA nanomaterials was performed by XRD analysis and the patterns of Mg-Al LDH and Mg-Al LDH–SA are presented in Fig. 1 . As can be seen from Fig. 1a, the XRD pattern of Mg-Al LDH, is in good agreement with those of the previously reported [34], which shows sharp and symmetric reflection peaks at (003) and (006) planes and broad and asymmetric reflections at (015) and (018) planes which is characteristic of layered structures. Moreover, the interlayer spacing values (d-spacing) calculated by (003) peak and Bragg's equation were 7.93 Å and 10.92 Å for Mg-Al-Cl LDH and Mg-Al LDH–SA, respectively. As a result, the intercalation of SA into the Mg-Al LDH layers was confirmed by increasing of the interlayer spacing and sensible shift of (003) plane to low angle (2Ɵ = 11.43°→ 8.09°) in the XRD pattern of Mg-Al LDH–SA (Fig. 1b).

The FT-IR spectra of Mg-Al LDH, SA molecule, and Mg-Al LDH–SA are presented in Fig. 2 . In the Mg-Al LDH (Fig. 2a), a broad band at 3474 cm−1 indicates the stretching vibration of the OH groups or physically adsorbed water molecules. The peaks at 1638 cm−1 may be attributed to the bending vibration of HOH of the adsorbed interlayer water molecules [32]. Two bands at 600 and 428 cm−1 can be assigned to the lattice vibrations of metal-oxygen and metal-hydroxyl bonds [34]. As can be seen from Fig. 2b pure SA presents many absorption peaks. A strong band at 1656 cm−1 is related to mode ν (CO) of the acid group [32,36]. Furthermore, a broad band in the 2400–3400 cm−1 region is attributed to ν (OH) moiety and ν (=CH) is observed at higher wavenumber values than 3000 cm−1. The bands due to ν(CC) of the aromatic ring are appeared at 1612, 1484, and 1444 cm−1, while in plane and out of plane δ(CH) bands are found below 1000 cm−1. The bands at 1295 cm−1 and 1248 cm−1 are attributed to CO and OH vibration, respectively [37]. The FT-IR spectrum after the incorporation of the SA into the interlayers of Mg-Al LDH is given in Fig. 2c. As can be seen, it contains both the characteristic peaks of pure SA and the typical peaks of the LDH, confirming the intercalation of SA anions into the interlayer galleries of the LDH. As salicylic acid is intercalated in the anionic form, the band attributable to CO vibration of the acid (1656 cm−1) has vanished. However, the bands at 1567 cm−1, 1460 cm−1, and 1364 cm−1 are related to the asymmetric and symmetric carboxylate stretching mode of the salicylate anions. Furthermore, the bands at 1264 cm−1 and 1147 cm−1 are assigned to the CC stretch in the aromatic ring and a band corresponding to metal-oxygen is detected at 593 cm−1 [37].

EDS analysis results of Mg-Al-Cl LDH and Mg-Al LDH–SA nanomaterials are presented in Table 1 . It can be seen that Mg: Al ratio (4.02: 1.00) in the synthesized nanomaterial was closely similar to the theoretical molar ratio (Mg: Al = 4: 1). The existence of the high amount of carbon in the Mg-Al LDH–SA is signified the intercalation of SA into the interlayer space of Mg-Al-Cl LDH. FE-SEM images of Mg-Al LDH–SA nanomaterial at 75000 × m (up) and 150000 × m (bottom) magnifications are shown in Fig. 3 . It shows an aggregate caused by the collection of thin flake- and hexagonal-like nanometric size crystallites that are expected for the synthesized layered material. Moreover, the hydrodynamic size of Mg-Al LDH–SA in the aqueous suspension was measured using DLS analysis. As can be seen from Fig. 4 a, Mg-Al LDH–SA exhibits a particle size distribution between 50 and 105 nm with an average hydrodynamic diameter of 65 nm and a polydispersity index of 0.461 (PDI = 0.461). As the PDI value is less than 0.5, so the stability of the prepared Mg-Al LDH–SA particles in the aqueous suspension is relatively good. Zeta potential or layer charge of LDHs is an important factor affecting the morphology of the particles and their interlayer spacing [38]. Furthermore, the dispersion and aggregation of the particles in the suspension are related to their zeta potential [39]. As shown in Fig. 4b, the zeta potential of Mg-Al LDH–SA is +32.0 mV. Therefore, it can be concluded that the majority of salicylate anions prefer to intercalate between the LDH layers instead of adsorption on the surface of the LDH, and Mg-Al LDH–SA nanomaterial has been successfully prepared.

To appraise the ability of the presented method for the determination of Fe3+ ions in human serum, the effect of some factors such as pH, initial SA concentration, amount of nanomaterial and reaction time were studied and optimized by a one-at-a-time method. The relative fluorescence intensity was used to assess the impact of the under exploration parameters on the determination of Fe3+ ions using the presented method. A 50 μmol L−1 Fe3+ ion solution was used for all the measurements and each trial was repeated at least three times.

The influence of pH on the difference in fluorescence intensity of Mg-Al LDH–SA sol solution in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ion (ΔF) was studied by applying the described procedure. The pH of the sample solution was adjusted with 0.1 mol L−1 HNO3 and/or NaOH solutions in the range between 3 and 9. It should be mentioned that the pH values less than 3 were not investigated because of solubility of LDH nanomaterials in strongly acidic media and protonation of salicylate anions in these pH values due to relatively high acid ionization constant of SA (pKa = 2.98) [40]. As can be seen from Fig. 5 a, ΔF is constant in the range of 3–7 and then decreases may be due to competing OH− with salicylate anions and also hydrolysis of Fe3+ ions at higher pH values. Therefore, pH 7 was selected as the optimum working pH, which is conceivable for the biological and environmental applications. The effect of the initial SA concentration in the synthesis procedure on the difference in fluorescence intensity was studied in the range of 0.001–200 mg L−1. As it can be seen from Fig. 5b, a linear relationship between ΔF and concentration of SA is observed in the range of 0.001–100 mg L−1 and after that reaches a constant value. Hence, 100 mg L−1 SA solution was chosen for further experiments.

The fluorescence signal in various amounts of Mg-Al LDH–SA nanomaterial was examined in the range of 1–5 mg of nanomaterial. Based on the attained results, the maximum fluorescence intensity difference was achieved in the range between 2 and 4 mg of the nanomaterial (data not shown). However, the fluorescence intensity decreases when higher than 4 mg of the nanomaterial is used, probably due to scattering of light by the nanomaterial and thus reducing fluorescence efficiency. Therefore, 2 mg of the nanomaterial was recommended for subsequent experiments. To investigate the influence of the reaction time, 2 mg of the nanomaterial was added into 3 mL of the sample solution containing 50 μmol L−1 Fe3+ ion and sonicated at room temperature at different times (1–20 min) before recording of the fluorescence intensity. This step was repeated for the blank solution, and the difference in fluorescence intensity was plotted versus reaction time. According to the obtained results, the ΔF increased with the increase in reaction time up to 5 min and then reached a constant value (data not shown). Therefore, the reaction time of 5 min was selected for further experiments. The effect of temperature on the fluorescence signal was investigated in the range of 20–40°C. It was found that the fluorescence intensity difference remained constant in all temperatures. Therefore, 25 °C was chosen for later experiments.

To demonstrate the utility of Mg-Al LDH–SA nanomaterial as a fluorescent sensor of Fe3+ ion, the competition experiments were carried out in the presence of Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Na+, K+, Ag+ and Al3+ ions. For this purpose, different quantities of the ions were added to the test solutions containing 50 μmol L−1 of Fe3+ ions and then followed according to the general procedure. An ion was considered as interference when its presence made a variation in the fluorescence intensity of the sample greater than ±5%. The results are summarized in Table 2 . It can be seen that the presence of most cations has no noticeable impact on the determination of Fe3+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the Fe3+ ion detection
ce:section.ce:para:
It is well known that fluorescence is favored in molecules with rigid planner structures. Thus, the fluorescence intensity of SA is enhanced by its intercalating into LDHs [40]. Because, SA is immobilized within interlayers of the LDHs, leading to a decrease in interaction between SA and the solvent [40,41]. On the other hand, fluorescence quenching typically refers to nonradiative energy transfer from excited species to other molecules through several mechanisms include; dynamic (collisional), static and long-range quenching [38]. Fig. 6 shows the fluorescence emission spectra of Mg-Al-Cl LDH, SA solution, Mg-Al LDH–SA and Mg-Al LDH–SA in the presence of Fe3+ ions. As can be seen from Fig. 6d, the fluorescence intensity of SA is enhanced in the presence of the Mg-Al LDH. This enhancement may be related to decreasing in probable collision quenching process that usually occurs in the aqueous solutions due to the low aggregation degree of SA in the LDH gallery leading to an increase in the fluorescence quantum yield. In this study, the fluorescence quantum yields of SA and Mg-Al LDH–SA were calculated using tyrosine as a reference. Based on the experimental results, the quantum yield of SA was increased from 18.2% to 86.5% in the presence of the Mg-Al LDH. However, the improved fluorescence intensity of Mg-Al LDH–SA was decreased in the presence of Fe3+ ions (Fig. 6c). The fluorescence quenching mechanism of the Mg-Al LDH–SA nanomaterial by Fe3+ ion may demonstrate by the nonradiative deactivation caused by the formation of a complex between SA and Fe3+ ions due to its high complex formation constant value at 25°C (Kf = 2.73 × 1017) [42]. Fig. 7 a demonstrates the inhibiting effect of different amounts of Fe3+ ions on the improved fluorescence intensity of Mg-Al LDH–SA. The efficiency of fluorescence quenching by a quenching species follows the Stern–Volmer equation:
(2) F0/F = 1+Ksv [Q]

Where F0 and F denote the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of quenching species (Fe3+ ions), respectively, [Q] represents the concentration of the quencher (mol L−1) and Ksv is Stern–Volmer quenching constant (L−1 mol). As presented in Fig. 7b, the plot of F0/F versus [Q] provides a good linear range over the Fe3+ ion concentration from 0.07 to 100 μmol L−1 with the intercept of 1.0377 and Ksv = 1.27 (±0.06) × 104 L mol−1. As the Ksv value is high, we guesstimated that quenching process may obey from the static quenching mechanism in which the quencher and fluorescent species form a stable complex in the ground state and fluorescence signal is only observed from the unbound fluorescent species immobilized within interlayers of the Mg-Al LDH. Another way to distinguish between dynamic and static quenching mechanisms is the study of temperature effect on Ksv. In the case of dynamic quenching, Ksv increases with increasing of the temperature, while the variation of the temperature does not effect on Ksv in static quenching mechanism. For this purpose, Stern–Volmer graphs were plotted at two different temperatures; 298 K and 313 K. Based on the results that are represented in Fig. 7b, Ksv values at 298 K and 313 K are 1.27 (±0.06) × 104 L mol−1 and 1.16 (±0.11) × 104 L mol−1, respectively. It was found that there is no significant difference between the obtained results at a 95% confidence level utilizing the Student t-test. Accordingly, in this study, the quenching process obeys from the static quenching mechanism.

In the optimal conditions, the calibration curve was constructed on three different days by plotting the difference in fluorescence intensity of Mg-Al LDH–SA nanomaterial in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ions (ΔF) versus concentration of Fe3+ ions in the range between 0.07 and 500 μmol L−1 and the mean of three curves was evaluated for validation studies. According to the obtained results, there is a good linear relationship between the ΔF and concentration of Fe3+ ions in the range of 0.07–100 μmol L−1 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9989. The regression equation is ΔF = 5.745C
(Fe) + 23.488, where ΔF is the fluorescence intensity difference of Mg-Al LDH–SA in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ion and C
(Fe) is Fe3+ ion concentration (μmol L−1). The limit of detection (LOD) of the method based on 3S
b /m, where S
b and m are the standard derivation of the blank and slope of calibration curve, respectively, was found to be 26 nmol L−1. To test the reproducibility of the synthesis of Mg-Al LDH–SA nanomaterial, the procedure was repeated five times. Then, 2.0 mg of Mg-Al LDH–SA nanomaterial obtained from each batch was individually added into 3 mL of deionized water and the after sonication for 5 min at room temperature, the fluorescence emission spectrum was recorded and compared with each other. It was found that the fluorescence intensities at λem = 404 nm and λex = 287 nm are the same with the precision of 3.2%. Additionally, the effect of 50 μmol L−1 Fe3+ ion on the fluorescence intensity of Mg-Al LDH–SA obtained from different batches was also studied in the optimum experimental conditions. The %RSD (n = 5) of the fluorescence intensities at 404 nm was found to be 4.8%. To investigate the repeatability of the method a series of six sample solutions containing 50 μmol L−1 Fe3+ ion were analyzed at the same and different days under the optimized experimental conditions for both intra-day and inter-day variations, respectively. The intra- and inter-day relative standard deviations (%RSD, n = 6) were found to be 3.4% and 4.5%, respectively. These findings were compared with those of other nanomaterial-based methods for determination of Fe3+ ion in different matrices. The related data listed in Table 3 reveals that the presented method has relatively low LOD, wide linear range and simple procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Method validation and analysis of real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility and applicability of the presented method in the determination of the Fe3+ ion in real samples, several human serum samples from persons with various levels of Fe3+ ion, who did not take any medicine at least 72 h prior to sampling, were analyzed by the presented method and the results are summarized in Table 4 . To confirm the accuracy of the presented method, atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) was applied as a standard method for the determination of the analyte in human serum. As the level of Fe3+ ion in human serum is relatively high, AAS method has been accepted as a conventional method for determination of iron in human serum samples [2]. No significant difference was found between the obtained results by the presented and standard method at a 95% confidence level using the paired t-test (Table 4). In addition, to evaluate the accuracy of the presented method, recovery experiments were also performed by spiking the deproteinized human serum samples with different amounts of Fe3+ ions, and the relative recovery values between 98.7% and 104.6% were acquired. Also, the standard addition method with nitrate salt of Fe3+ as the standard solution was used for quantification of the unknown amount of Fe3+ ion in human serum. The fluorescence emission spectra of the deproteinized human serum samples before and after standard additions, along with the related calibration curve, and Stern–Volmer plot for the quenching of Mg-Al LDH–SA fluorescence by Fe3+ ions in the human serum medium are given in Fig. 8 . It was found that there is no significant difference between the results from the standard addition method and common calibration curve with regard to the Fe3+ ion concentration level in the human serum at a 95% confidence level applying the Student t-test. On the other hand, according to the Fig. 8III, Ksv value was found to be 1.23 (±0.08) × 104 L mol−1. So, there is a good agreement between the results obtained from the Stern–Volmer plots in deionized water and human serum media. As a result, the developed method is free from the matrix interferences of human serum samples. Moreover, to study the stability of the prepared Mg-Al LDH–SA nanomaterial, fluorescence intensity variation of the nanomaterial as a function of illumination time was recorded under 287 nm excitation for 20 min. It was found that the fluorescence intensity remains unchanged during the time scanning process and possesses excellent stability. These results demonstrate that the presented method has a great potential for the determination of Fe3+ ion in biological samples.

In this work, a novel and sensitive “turn-offˮ fluorescent chemosensor based on Mg-Al LDH–SA nanomaterial was presented for selective detection of Fe3+ ions in human serum samples and the results are consistent with those found with the standard AAS method. The established method is based on the ability of Fe3+ ions to reduce the fluorescence signal of Mg-Al LDH–SA nanomaterial with increasing of Fe3+ ion concentration because of the formation of a stable complex between Fe3+ ions and salicylic acid. The possible fluorescence quenching mechanism was discussed, and based on the experimental results quenching process obeys from the static quenching mechanism. The presented method provides the fast, accurate and reliable determination of iron in human serum with low operation cost and simplicity of instrumentation. Other advantages of this method include easiness of synthesis of the nanomaterial, wide linear range, low detection limit and environmentally friendly. The developed method also has good precision and selectivity, which makes it an appropriate analytical method for routine applications in biomedical analysis laboratories.
There are no conflicts to declare.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from the Research Council of Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University (Grant no. ASMU/96372-17) is gratefully acknowledged. The authors also thank the Iranian Blood Transfusion Research Center (Tabriz, Iran) and Danesh Pathobiology and Genetic Lab. for donating human serum samples.
